source.notes: Optimus _ Joe Gramc - 2026_05_26 10_29 EDT - Notes by Gemini.docx

call_summary: Max Riemer (Optimus) ran an introductory demo for Drew Cherba, a consultant sitting in for FiveStar's Joe Gramc, who no-showed. Max clearly framed Optimus as a 'shipper intelligence platform built for sales' with a concrete proof point and asked solid discovery questions, but the pitch stayed generic to FiveStar's Northeast Ohio freight and ended with only a vague, undated follow-up. Drew was intellectually engaged and cast himself as the eventual implementer, yet repeatedly described himself as a peripheral observer and made no commitment.

delivery_engagement.explanation: Max was responsive and conversational rather than scripted—he adapted to Drew's summaries, dug into FiveStar's stack, and openly flagged uncertainty about a competitor rather than bluffing; acoustically he was slightly below his own baseline on pitch variation but steadier on volume, well within normal range.

evidence.quote: Can you give me the rundown of the current tools or data that y'all are
we were told last week by another prospect that they were literally deciding between us and gen logs was that they are also getting factoring data

value_prop_clarity.explanation: A neutral listener could restate the value in one sentence—verified shipper data (lanes, volumes, commodities, contacts) so reps make specific calls instead of generic outreach—and Max backed it with a concrete, quantified proof point and a clear competitive differentiator.

evidence.quote: I would say it is a shipper intelligence platform built for sales
within the first seven days a dude went and ran 14 loads with them in the first month
they do only have cameras on highways and interstates. So they don't know where a shipment picked up or where it got delivered

relevance.explanation: The pitch remained a generic product tour—Drew supplied all the FiveStar specifics (Northeast Ohio hub, local spiderweb strategy, two reps, SalesFlare/expired Apollo/Gen Logs), but Max never connected Optimus to FiveStar's actual lanes, region, or freight network, only lightly referencing their rep/account-manager structure at the very end.

evidence.quote: when you call someone and you're able to pinpoint something specific like a high volume DC or like a specific lane
How many sales reps do y'all have?

discovery_progression.explanation: Max asked productive questions about tools, team structure, and competitor usage, but the call ended with no concrete dated next step—he deferred to chasing the absent Joe with a vague timeframe and failed to lock any commitment from Drew, the very person who said he would drive adoption.

evidence.quote: how's Gen Logs been on the sales side? Do y'all use it for like procurement
I'll reach out to Joe and then we'll try and get something else here maybe this week

Max Riemer.coaching_action: Before ending the call, lock a specific dated next step with whoever is actually in the room—here, book a concrete follow-up demo on the calendar with Drew (your likely implementer/champion) and secure a time for Joe, instead of leaving it at 'I'll reach out to Joe... maybe this week.'

signals.signal: Genuine, own-words enthusiasm for the positioning
signals.quote: I like that. Shipper intelligence built for sales. Okay.

signals.signal: Question depth—actively qualifying how the data is ethically sourced
signals.quote: that's kind of the I guess the legit ethical way that you were able to get your hands on the documents was you were um providing a service to the factoring companies

signals.signal: Positions himself as the future implementer/champion
signals.quote: he's not the one that's going to end up you know getting the sales reps to implement it. That's probably going to be my job

signals.signal: Distances himself and makes no commitment or buyer-owned next step
signals.quote: I am I'm kind of an a an observer slash like partial implementer
